data.title: Ford's Highly Touted BEV Czar Leaves, His Exit Won't Quickly Change Things
data.description:
The departure of Doug Field, who was recruited to supervise Ford Motor Company’s (F) BEV development, comes amidst the automaker’s struggles to prove it can match its top rivals, including Tesla (TSLA) and a slew of Chinese concerns whose BEVs are gobbling global market share.

The reason or reasons for Field’s exit have not been spelled out explicitly by him or by the company.

On Field’s watch, Ford has produced a new Universal Electric Vehicle platform due out next year as the basis of a $30,000 midsize pickup truck – and eventually a number of other models. Ford’s first BEVs, the Mustang Mach-e and F-150 Lightning full-size pickup, developed prior to Fields, have largely disappointed. Weak sales and pricing have contributed to steep losses and writedowns as a result of its first attempt to electrify its fleet.

Despite record total revenue of $187.3 billion (driven by the profitable Ford Pro and Ford Blue divisions), EV-related charges and losses caused Ford to swing from a $5.9 billion net profit in 2024 to a $8.2 billion net loss for 2025.

Big earner

Ford’s large pickup truck, the gasoline-powered F150 and its variants, provides the bulk of earnings for the automaker, as it has for many years. The Bronco line of rugged SUVs and the Explorer have been successful, to a lesser extent, while middle of the road models such as the Ford Escape have been dropped from the lineup.

Field, 61, who began his career at Ford before migrating to Tesla (where he worked on development of the Model 3) and Apple (AAPL), which ultimately dropped its vehicle development ambitions. Ford hired Field to lead digital, software, and electrification efforts in 2021. Mach-e and Lightning were both in production upon his arrival, at which point it was becoming clear that both models were too costly and technologically uncompetitive.

In early 2024, CEO Jim Farley revealed that a small elite Ford development team under Field had been posted to the West Coast since 2022. The mission: Creating a low-cost, flexible platform for a family of BEVs, starting with a medium-sized pickup. The so-called skunkworks has evolved into the Advanced Development Products team in Long Beach, Calif., presumably an attractive venue for young, tech-savvy engineers and designers famously uninterested in relocating to Ford’s engineering center in Dearborn, Mich.

Additionally, Ford operates a research and innovation center in Palo Alto, employing about 300 engineers and scientists.

Coincident with Field’s exit, announced by Farley on April 16 and portrayed as voluntary, is the creation of a new division called Product Creation and Industrialization that includes the group formerly led by Field. The division will be run by Ford COO Kumar Galhotra, while Alan Clarke will lead the Advanced Development Projects team that created the Universal platform.

Ford’s latest moves are emblematic of the incumbent global auto industry’s difficult migration toward the software-defined vehicle (SDV) and away from its roots building mechanical and electro-mechanical conveyances. Abrupt changes in U.S. government policy on top of consumer resistance to BEVs have complicated and added cost and delays to the automaker’s strategy for evolving its vehicle models toward an electrified future.

Unlike Tesla and modern Chinese BEVs, Ford and other incumbents have relied for decades on electronic architectures that use heavy copper wiring and scores of individual dedicated electronic control units, each with its own software, for features such as windshield wipers, entertainment clusters, engines, and lighting. Integrating the software programs with one another from multiple suppliers has been a nightmare and a contributing factor to Ford’s recalls and quality problems.

Tesla and others have designed their architectures with fewer, larger central computers that run homegrown software programs, each controlling a specific zone and networked to vehicle features with lightweight wiring.

Last April, Ford killed its project to create a similar next-generation central computing and wiring architecture, citing ballooning costs and delays, sources told Reuters.

A spokesperson explained, "We are committed to delivering fully connected vehicle experiences across our entire lineup, regardless of powertrain, while many others in the industry are bringing the most advanced tech only to electric vehicles." The statement suggests the development of a different electronic architecture that will function in BEVs as well as internal combustion powered models such as the Ford F-150 pickup.

Software headaches

Ford’s retreat hinted at the inherent difficulties, common to incumbent automotive manufacturers, of developing world-class in-house digital and software expertise.

The advent of over-the-air (OTA) software updates in automobiles – similar to the routine updates used in smartphones – enables improvements and on/off switching to features such as self-driving and heated seats or sunroofs. Wireless connectivity between vehicles and manufacturers is expected to provide a substantial additional revenue stream as well. In theory, according to a McKinsey study, the automotive software and electronics market could reach $462 billion by 2030 and grow at a 5.5% compound annual rate.

Ford’s Blue Cruise hands-free driving feature, for example, currently sells for $50 a month; Tesla’s full self-driving technology yields $99 a month. Ford has 1.2 million Blue Cruise customers; Tesla, has 1.1 million self-driving customers.

For at least the past decade, the performance of Ford shares has been lackluster, barely moving in price from a narrow depressed range, with brief periods on the upside and downside. Nothing about Ford’s creation of a new BEV platform suggests an imminent breakout from this trend – at least not until and unless the midsize BEV pickup truck proves to be a sales and marketing success.

Cash for now

Ford’s current dividend yield on common shares of 4.75% is decent, though the safety is rated D+ by SeekingAlpha; the payout has shown to be vulnerable during periods of Ford’s financial weakness – of which lately Ford seems to be on the brink.

I wish the Ford blue oval every success – this American icon has played a key role over the last century in putting the country on wheels and establishing the nation as a pillar of global industrial strength. Gaining a foothold in the world of digital tech and electrification remains a significant hurdle. From an investment point of view, the shares still look too risky for anything other than a small, perhaps sentimental, plunge on the chance that the Dearborn, Mich., brain trust can turn things around.

F remains a Hold.

data.title: Medtronic: Is This Dividend Aristocrat Finally A "Buy" Before Earnings?
data.description:
Medtronic plc (MDT) operates in the medical devices industry sector. It was founded in 1949 and is a very well-established business, considering its peers/competitors are the likes of: Boston Scientific (BSX), Abbott (ABT), Stryker (SYK), Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), and Thermo Fisher Scientific (TMO). They operate out of both Dublin, Ireland, and Minneapolis, MN.

I covered Medtronic previously, in November 2025, and gave a Hold rating. While praising its decent dividend yield at the time (around 3%), long history of dividend growth, and industry stalwart status, I was less impressed with their low single-digit revenue growth rates, modest returns on invested capital over the years, and what seemed to me like an unreasonably high valuation, based on growth rates (PEG ratio around 2.5). If it were not for the company’s strong product line and history of innovation, exceptional reputation for quality, solid industry position, and consistent FCF generation over the years, I could have easily rated the stock a Sell. Indeed, I think for long-term investors, this would be considered a fairly low-risk equity holding.

In any event, I offered to upgrade it to a Buy rating if the share price dropped by 30%. Alas, perhaps unsurprisingly, the stock has bounced around within a tight range but, overall, fallen around 12% since then, versus a 4% gain for the broader market.

The company starts its 2027 fiscal year on May 1 and is set to release Q4 and full year 2026 earnings on June 3. As such, I believe it is an opportune time to assess progress in the key areas of development mentioned in my first coverage, and to provide an update on my expectations for the company and the stock.

An Update on Progress

Medtronic have an enormous suite of products that it sells to hospitals and physician/health centers. These are its primary customers. This diversity in their product catalogue has the advantage of providing dependable income and FCF, but also means any new products are unlikely to move the needle in terms of revenue generation. In Q3 2026, their last quarter, organic revenue growth was 6%. The company provided the following revenue mix and geographical break-down:

Revenue growth has been in a fairly tight range for several years. I notice, however, that revenue growth has begun to pick up over the last few quarters (on a TTM basis). Are they beginning to grow into their valuation?

Investors should keep their expectations for double-digit revenue growth in check. Moreover, EPS growth rates have actually declined over the last few quarters.

In my view, Medtronic are simply too big a company, and the competition between their peers, mentioned above, too intense, for us to expect a rapid or even significant upswing in revenue or EPS. The company may have competitive advantages in some areas, but these would be very specific to a particular product or category, and certainly not across their entire portfolio. While their gross margins have remained consistent, around 64-68% (currently 64.9%), high overhead costs have kept their net profit margin between 10-17% over recent years (currently 13.1%). Margins have remained stable, which suggests some pricing power and a strong market presence. Given these parameters, the company should be rated, in terms of its valuation, as a stalwart or slow grower. This would demand a fairly low earnings multiple, but I’ll assess this later.

Since my last coverage, the company have announced several new developments in their pipeline. I’ll focus on the key developments, highlighted by management.

Cardiac Ablations Solutions

Medtronic highlighted Cardiac Ablation Solutions (CAS) in its Q3 2026 earnings call. Revenue in this segment increased 80%, including 137% in the U.S., on the strength of its PFA (pulsed field ablation) portfolio. In my first coverage, I noted the excitement expressed by the CEO Geoff Martha about the revenue ramp expected from their two leading products in this category: the Affera Mapping System, and the Sphere-9 PF/RF catheter.

This area of healthcare has some secular tailwinds behind it, with an aging population, coupled with the increasing prevalence of heart diseases like atrial fibrillation and cardiovascular conditions more generally. Medtronic’s PulseSelect and Boston Scientific’s Farapulse PFA lead the market as the first two systems, approved in Dec 2023 and Jan 2024 respectively. This was followed by Varipulse (approved Nov 2024) by Johnson & Johnson’s subsidiary Biosense Webster. Medtronic’s Affera Mapping System Sphere-9 catheter were approved in October 2024, but have performed very well in this crowded space. Abbott received the FDA nod for its Volt system at the end of last year to add to the mix. Each claims to be unique and superior, but it will likely take time for a clear leader to emerge, if it ever does. In some markets with little to differentiate products based on performance, fragmentation could remain the key characteristic and eroding pricing power could result. In any event, the initial revenue ramp of Medtronic’s system has been very encouraging.

Medtronic not only generate revenue from the initial system sales (Affera bundle) but also through the disposables (Sphere 9); the latter provides recurring revenue that increases as the installed base grows. The information below is taken from their website.

This successful product launch likely represents the biggest growth driver for Medtronic presently, and with approvals likely in other locations soon (e.g., Japan), the revenue ramp is likely to continue over the coming year or so.

The CV segment of Medtronic’s business generated the largest proportion of revenue over the TTM period ($13.5 billion of $35.5 billion total revenue), and this proportion is likely to grow further. Management noted during the Q3 2026 call that it gained 4% market share in the past year, which suggests it is competing well against its rivals. Medtronic’s Cardiac Rhythm & Heart Failure revenue was the fastest growing business segment over the TTM period, growing 14%.

The HUGO and Stealth AXIS Robot-assisted Surgical Systems

Medtronic received FDA approval for their HUGO robotic-assisted surgery system, which performed its first operation in Cleveland a few weeks ago. The company consider this a major breakthrough, alongside its recent FDA approval for its Stealth AXIS robotic system for spinal surgery, which also received FDA approval in February. Both were highlighted as part of their latest investor presentation. I’ll examine them together as they occupy the same space.

HUGO

The HUGO system will compete directly with the da Vinci system by Intuitive Surgical (ISRG), which has been the gold-standard robotic-assisted surgery platform in the U.S. and globally since its launch over 25 years ago, going through constant innovations and enhancements. It is now in its 5th iteration, and has broad market dominance.

The da Vinci controls 60% of the market according to this detailed analysis – 3.1 million procedures were performed using its systems in 2025 – and it is unclear how HUGO will compete, and therefore whether it will provide a sufficient return on Medtronic’s investment developing it. Comparative analysis across the several sites and research studies I looked at suggests that HUGO is no better than da Vinci. This study found comparable data in terms of operative time, estimated patient blood loss, and post-operative complications. This study found comparable surgical outcomes, but the da Vinci system necessitated shorter set-up and console phase durations; there were fewer malfunctioning events; and the system generated higher user satisfaction scores. This review site suggests some technical distinctions (below) but concludes that the da Vinci system ‘currently holds a significant technological edge’, stemming from the system’s widespread adoption and its ‘extensive range of instruments and accessories’. I agree.

According to this comparative analysis, competition in this space "might lead to longer selling cycles for all players as physicians prefer to have options," but without HUGO demonstrating a discernible improvement or advantage over the da Vinci, I am not convinced that it will take significant market share. Not only does da Vinci have a much larger installed base, more committed and trained physicians using its systems, a more recognizable and trusted name/brand, a track-record of innovation, extremely high customer retention built off long-standing relationships, and high user satisfaction, it is also approved to treat far more surgical procedures. The list includes:

  • Bariatric surgery for weight-loss: gastric bypass surgery and sleeve gastrectomy
  • Cardiac surgery
  • Colorectal surgery: colon and rectal resections, especially used in cancer patients
  • General surgery: inguinal/ventral hernia repair, gallbladder surgery (cholecystectomy), surgery for gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), and surgery of the liver and pancreas.
  • Gynecological surgery: Hysterectomy, endometriosis resection, myomectomy (fibroid tumor removal), and pelvic organ prolapse repair.
  • Head and neck surgery: Transoral robotic surgery for mouth/throat tumors
  • Thoracic surgery: lung surgery (lobectomy) and lung biopsy, alongside surgeries in the esophagus or thymus gland.
  • Urology surgery: Prostatectomy (for prostate cancer), bladder cancer surgery (cystectomy), kidney cancer surgery (partial nephrectomy), adrenal cancer, and testicular cancer.

Presently, the HUGO system is only approved by the FDA to perform the following surgical procedures: prostatectomy, nephrectomy, and cystectomy. This puts them at a massive disadvantage, with the da Vinci system having a moat around its business based on high entry costs – the costs of developing tools/instruments for other surgical procedures, R&D, approval process, commercialization – and high switching costs. A da Vinci system costs around $2 million and performs a plethora of different procedures. What would compel a cash-strapped hospital to purchase a HUGO system, force their staff to retrain themselves, so that it could perform fewer procedures? Medtronic would likely have to target new facilities that have yet to choose a robot-assisted surgical system, and trust that the physician will recommend a system that performs fewer surgeries and has no competitive advantages. Perhaps they may undercut them on cost, but I simply don’t see why a hospital would take that risk, given the long-standing trust that the da Vinci system has built for itself over the years.

Therefore, in my estimation, Intuitive Surgical’s moat is too wide for HUGO to breach. I’d be surprised if it took more than 10% of the market.

Stealth AXIS

As for Stealth AXIS, it also enters a crowded market, with Zimmer Biomet (ZBH) and its Rosa platform and Stryker and its MAKO system already more established. Knee and hip surgeries are in fact the main procedures completed by these two, but both are, or at least have been, used on the spine. Rosa received FDA approval for use on the spine in 2019, but I could find little information on its current website about these procedures. It is possible that spinal use has been discontinued for the Rosa system.

According to Stryker, just 10% of spine surgeries use robotics, which presents a long runway for growth. The MAKO Spine system does seem to be currently used for spinal procedures. It offers certain advantages for the surgeon, enhancing accuracy and precision. The extended indication of MAKO for spinal surgery received FDA approval in 2024.

However, what seems to distinguish the AXIS system is that is offers “live” navigation – using what it calls a “LiveAlign” sentimental tracking feature – that adapts to a patient’s movement during the surgery. According to MassDevice, it

enables surgeons to visualize anatomic motion, surgical adjustments and patient alignment in real time during spine surgery. They can accomplish this without the need for repeated imaging.

Medtronic called this an “industry-first capability”; a tenured professor of Orthopaedic Surgery (Neurological Surgery) at Columbia University and Medtronic consultant, Dr. Ronald A. Lehman, Jr., described it as “truly game changing technology.” As a non-expert, I certainly would not disagree with its potential in this space, given what appears to be some technical advantages that it offers, helping to reduce procedure time.

Beyond spinal procedures, news came in late March of FDA approval for the Stealth AXIS system for extended indications in cranial and ENT procedures. According to the press release, the system’s advantages were repeated:

A defining capability of the [AXIS] platform is its AI-enabled architecture, which supports advanced planning and visualization before, during, and after surgery. For cranial procedures, the system includes AI-based automatic tractography that helps surgeons generate patient-specific brain maps and visualize critical neural pathway associated with essential brain functions … Additionally, the system's flexible architecture is designed to support continuous software innovation, expanded capabilities, and deeper ecosystem connectivity to help shape the next era of surgery.

Investors may be growing weary of companies outside of the technology space claiming they are incorporating AI into their systems for enhanced efficiencies, but it makes sense that AI would be incorporated into the healthcare sector more generally, and surgery specifically, given the enormous amount of data required for precision operative tasks. It seems logical that the more information obtained for a surgeon to use, the greater efficacy and safety as an outcome.

If I understand its capabilities correctly, this would certainly suggest a competitive advantage for the Stealth AXIS system over its competitors. The system may help to stimulate growth in the company’s Neuroscience segment, which was the second largest contributor to revenue last year but saw just 2.5% organic growth. In Q3 2026 earnings, management highlighted 9% revenue growth in spine care, singling out the Stealth AXIS as a growth catalyst.

These developments are exciting to me, and I look forward to management’s update in June. Investors should listen for management commentary on the physician take-up and commercialization/sales ramp.

The MiniMed 780G Automated Insulin System

On a TTM basis, Medtronic’s Diabetes segment generated nearly $800 million in revenue. This has been led by their MiniMed systems and solutions for diabetes patients. Since my last coverage, they have seen progress made in developing, launching, and commercializing this system.

In December, they launched in the U.S., integrated with Instinct sensor, an Abbott (ABT)-based CGM. According to the press release, the

Instinct sensor provides real-time glucose readings which the system uses to automate insulin adjustments every five minutes, helping people with diabetes spend less time managing their condition and more time living their life.

The system is approved for insulin control for Type 1 Diabetes patients seven years old and above. Medtronic describe the MiniMed 780G as their "most advanced automated insulin delivery system," which differentiates itself by integrating the Instinct sensor which

offers up to 15 days of wear, fewer insertions, and a slim, discreet design. It's the world's smallest and thinnest integrated CGM, providing comfort and convenience without compromising accuracy.

This news was quickly followed by FDA clearance of the associated app (MiniMed Go), which is intended to be integrated with the MiniMed Go Smart Multiple Daily Injection system, with its insulin pen and Abbott-developed Instinct sensor. Access expanded to Type 2 diabetes patients in February, with a further FDA approval, and a European launch post-approval also commenced in February.

Medtronic compete in this space with Dexcom (DXCM) and its Insulet Omnipod 5 system. They claim it superior on a head-to-head basis, but this independent comparison site offered a more balanced view, highlighting the fact that the Omnipod does not require tubing as a massive competitive advantage, based on patient convenience. The consumer market will ultimately decide. In any case, these developments are couched within the bigger story that Medtronic recently spun-off MiniMed as a stand-alone publicly-traded company. They filed Form S-1 registration statement with the SEC earlier in the year and MiniMed (MMED) IPO’d last month. It raised $560 million, with Medtronic immediately retaining 90% of the company. As a stand-alone entity, it will have greater autonomy with Medtronic able to streamline its resources better. I note, however, that since the launch, shares of MMED have declined from around $18.50 to $13.40, knocking about $2 billion off its market cap in a matter of weeks.

I have some mixed feelings about the move, but in general it makes good sense. I can see that Medtronic’s diabetes segment, though its smallest stand-alone segment, was also one of the strongest performers over the past year (growing 10.7% in revenue). The vast majority of this has now become part of MiniMed. Indeed, in filing its S-1, MiniMed reported 2025 revenue of $2.72 billion for 2025, closely mirroring Medtronic’s diabetes revenue for 2025 of $2.76 billion. If I understand it correctly, they are basically one and the same. MiniMed remains unprofitable and has struggled with declining gross, operating, and net margins over the period for which I have financial information. So, on this basis alone the divestiture makes sense. However, alongside revenue growth being decent, and above Medtronic’s broad average for 2025 ($3.6%), MiniMed also saw encouraging FCF margin improvement, up from (negative) -7.7% in 2023 to (negative) -1.95% in 2025.

So, while Medtronic retain 90% ownership of this company and remain tied to an unprofitable entity with lower gross margins, they may see some benefit in time with reductions in overheads costs (manufacturing, R&D, staffing, etc.) that could unlock some value as the new company finds its own footing and, hopefully, continues to see improvements in its FCF generation.

Financials and Valuation Metrics

In the Q3 call, management slightly increased guidance to a full-year 2026 amount of $35.1-35.2 billion. Using the midpoint ($35.15 billion), and subtracting revenue from the first 3 quarters, this means they are expecting revenue for Q4 2026 of $9.594 billion. This would represent a 7.47% revenue growth rate from Q4 2025 (at $8.927 billion). They might hit that, but I suspect revenue might be lower, and closer to their 5-6% range they have been anticipating for some time. Revenue from especially their PFA portfolio might surprise to the upside, as might they unit sales of AXIS, but I have less hope for HUGO given the intense competition in that market.

In my last coverage, I highlighted the problem the company had with its overhead costs. I note that S,G&A expenses increased more than R&D costs over the previous 4 quarters. The divestiture of their MiniMed business will likely help this, but management to my mind must show a more concerted effort to improve cash flow and provide a stronger ROIC. On a TTM basis, Medtronic has provided a ROIC around 5-8% over the past few years, as seen below.

However, this site has estimated that its WACC (weighted average cost of capital) is above this figure, at around 7.7%, based on cost of equity, tax rate, and cost of debt. Indeed, a key problem is the company’s high debt load and the value this destroys for shareholders when the hurdle rate (7.7%) is above their ROIC rate (6.78%). The company’s invested capital is not compounding value for shareholders, but destroying it, albeit slowly.

We can see from its financials that Medtronic have cash, equivalents and short-term investments of close to $8.4 billion, amidst total current assets of $24 billion. However, inventory represents $6.3 billion of this and rising. Their inventory turnover ratio is currently at 2, which is low. It has also been falling slowly but steadily over the past few years, down from 2.4 five years ago. They are struggling to shift their inventory, which is a drag on their balance sheet. Indeed, their inventory asset value was $4.3 billion in Q4 2021, that is $2 billion less.

Also on the asset side of its balance sheet is a goodwill impairment of $41.9 billion. The company have a track record of acquisitions. MiniMed (acquired in 2001) and Affera (2022) are two notable examples, and they recently closed on acquiring CathWorks to assist their cardiovascular segment and on Scientia Vascular to assist their neurovascular portfolio. The overpayment for these and other acquisitions, however, distorts their balance sheet by adding goodwill. Subtracting this figure, total assets are reduced to $50.4 billion, which is much closer to their total liabilities of $42.3 billion. Shareholders have also seen their equity slowly dissolve over time, down from $52.8 billion in Q1 2022 to $49.2 presently. It is certainly not the worst balance sheet I have seen of a company this size, but it could certainly be better.

I note that the company’s quick and current ratios have slightly increased since my last coverage, but of course rising inventory distorts current asset value.

The company have a large debt load, totaling $28.1 billion, of which almost all of it ($27.9 billion) is classed as long-term. Still, they have a net debt position of $19.7 billion ($15.29 per share). On a TTM basis, the company paid $712 million in interest payments on this debt. Their efforts to pay this down have been thwarted by the company taking on new debt every year for the past 4.

If companies are demonstrating strong capital deployment, through high ROIC, and delivering on improving margins, revenue growth and FCF generation, investors would (indeed should) be willing to pay a higher price for a company based on earnings multiple. However, Medtronic has been kind of stagnating, or at least not growing at a rate that would justify purchasing shares at a high P/E ratio.

I note in comparison to some of its peers that it currently trades at a P/E slightly below them, but when factoring growth (PEG ratio), the company is richly valued. I tend to avoid looking at companies that have a P/E more than double their EPS growth rate (a PEG above 2), and MDT is no exception. Medtronic is the worst of them all in this regard, despite having a high valuation “grade” offered by Seeking Alpha. I’m not sure why, but perhaps it factors in P/E ratio alone, and on that basis the stock looks cheap. As a GARP investor, I disagree.

The company also appears cheaper than its 5-year average for several reasons: falling EPS growth, declining FCF growth, falling margins across the board, and a worsening balance sheet with rising debt and increasing inventories. These are not the types of companies I have much interest in owning, even if it pays a 3.4% dividend yield, which is well covered.

Conclusion

Medtronic remains a well-managed company, with a strong product portfolio, extensive pipeline, and a history of product innovation in a space driven by durable secular growth and strong product demand. However, capital deployment has been questionable at times and shareholders have seen their value eroded, with high overhead costs, a massive debt load, and a lack of a clear plan to improve profitability. The company operates in several very competitive areas, but some of their products, like HUGO, which would have cost millions of dollars to develop, are unlikely to knock industry leaders like da Vinci from their pedestal. I expect their Stealth AXIS robotic system and PFA portfolio in CV health to represent growth drivers, but the company seems to be dragged down by other less profitable segments.

With the company anticipating over 7% revenue growth to meet the midrange of their guidance when they report full-year results in June, I am less convinced. They may hit the low end of their guidance and provide encouraging updates on their pipeline and recent product launch revenue ramps, but I remain skeptical of its valuation based on projected growth rates. Indeed, I do not think this is a company worth paying for at a 2.76 PEG ratio. Indeed, the PEG ratio was 2.5 back when I wrote my first article, and the share price has fallen 12% since then, which says something.

In terms of risk/reward, I see that there are other companies growing stronger and at cheaper valuations in this space, which might be better investments at this time. Johnson & Johnson, Boston Scientific, Stryker, and Abbott all have higher forward revenue growth rates and have higher FCF margins, according to Seeking Alpha data. On a P/E ratio basis, Medtronic is only slightly cheaper, which to my mind is not sufficient as a counterweight to offset slower growth.

I will continue with my Hold recommendation for the time being, until I see signs of a meaningful upswing in sales, margins, and returns on invested capital. Current investors with the dividend in mind should be rewarded here over the long term, but patience is required. At its present valuation, I’d need to see a further 20% drop or so in its share price to get me interested, but even then I wouldn’t invest on valuation alone.

data.title: Intel Q1: The Market Has Lost Its Mind
data.description:
Article Thesis

Intel Corporation (INTC) reported much stronger than expected Q1 earnings results on Thursday evening, which resulted in a huge positive share price reaction. This followed huge gains over the last year — although from a low level. Intel continues with its turnaround, but its valuation has gotten rather high — so right now may not be a good time to enter or expand a position in Intel.

Past Coverage

I have written about Intel Corporation here on Seeking Alpha in the past, most recently in September 2025, when I published this article. I was bullish on INTC back then on the back of a deal with Nvidia (NVDA), which has worked out very well so far — INTC has returned more than 150% since September when we factor in the post-earnings move on Thursday. With the company reporting its most recent earnings results for its fiscal first quarter, I want to update my views on the company today.

What Happened?

Following the market's close on Thursday, Intel Corporation released its Q1 results, with the headline numbers looking like this:

The company outperformed expectations on both lines — naturally, that's a very nice result, although it wasn't too much of a surprise, as the company had delivered a double beat during the previous two quarters as well. The market reacted very positively to Intel's results, sending shares higher by close to 20% at the time of writing — this means that shares are currently trading above the previous all-time high. Let's take a closer look.

Intel's Q1: Turnaround In Progress

Intel's revenues were higher than expected, but the growth rate of 7% wasn't quite as explosive as what one might expect based on the 15% share price jump in after-hours trading. On the other hand, investors got used to rather bad results from Intel in the past, and analysts were expecting a small revenue decline — so results were a lot better than expected, although INTC's growth is still way lower compared to that of many other chip companies.

Importantly, Intel beat its own guidance — it had forecasted significantly lower revenues in January. Companies and management teams that under-promise and overdeliver are a lot better compared to ones that do the contrary, I believe — and INTC's ability to exceed its own goals seems to be well-liked by the market, too.

Looking at the revenue performance across Intel's different business units, we see that Artificial Intelligence-exposed units did, not surprisingly, perform well: Intel's Data Center and AI unit saw its revenue expand by more than 20%, from $4.1 billion to $5.1 billion, which is a very nice growth rate, although still way below what Nvidia is doing with its data center business, despite NVDA growing from a much larger base. Of course, not every company can deliver Nvidia-like growth, and Intel's growth in this unit was appealing for sure.

Intel's client computing business fared worse, reporting more or less stable revenues versus one year earlier — not a disaster at all, but far from exciting. When we consider the rather weak consumer sentiment in many countries, as well as the energy price shock that started in Q1 and that likely made some consumers wary of buying higher-priced goods, the macro environment wasn't easy for the client computing unit, but Intel, overall, is still held back when its largest unit isn't growing at all.

Intel's foundry service showed some growth, although margins are still deeply negative, so investors don't really have anything from this growth for now, as it remains a big money loser — the foundry business actually lost $100 million more in Q1 2026 compared to one year earlier. Hopefully, this will change in the foreseeable future, as the revenue growth that is generated in the foundry business isn't really valuable otherwise.

Looking at the margin development of Intel's two other main business units, data center and AI and client computing, we see that the former did quite well: its segment operating margin more than doubled, from 14% to 31%, which is the result of both pricing power and operating leverage. While margins are still behind what competitors such as Nvidia achieve, operating margins of around 30% are far from unattractive, and the big margin increase in this business unit was a major driver of company-wide profits. Intel's client computing business reported a segment operating margin of 33% for the first quarter, which was up slightly from one year earlier. I like that the margin expanded a little — this indicates that INTC is doing well when it comes to cost controls — but between only some minor margin growth and stable revenues, there was not a lot of profit growth in the client computing unit.

In recent years, cash flows were an issue for Intel, as heavy investments and rather weak cash generation hurt its balance sheet. During the first quarter, Intel burned some cash as well: Operating cash flow was rather meager, at just $1 billion, and due to $5 billion in capital spending, free cash flow was negative by $2 billion after accounting for investments from joint venture partners worth $2 billion. While Intel isn't at risk financially, shareholders will likely not get any meaningful shareholder returns in the foreseeable future due to the still-weak free cash flow numbers.

Intel: What's the Outlook?

With its Q1 report, Intel also announced its expectations for the second quarter. Management sees revenues of $13.8 billion to $14.8 billion, which is a rather wide range, but going with the middle of that, Intel would grow at a low-double-digit rate. That would mean some growth acceleration versus the just-reported first quarter and a major improvement versus the last couple of years — a 9.7% revenue increase in late 2023 was the best INTC had to offer since 2021. If Intel hits its own forecasts, that would be a sign that the current turnaround continues to progress — and based on the recent history, INTC may even exceed its own guidance once more, which would, of course, be even better.

Management forecasts a gross margin of 39% for Q2, which would be a bit below the 41% we saw in Q1 — hopefully, this is just Intel's management being conservative again, as a quarter-to-quarter margin decline would be unfortunate. Even if Intel doesn't exceed its guidance, it seems pretty clear that Q2 2026 will be way better than Q2 2025, though — the guidance implies a gross margin improvement of almost 1,000 basis points year-over-year.

Is Intel A Good Investment?

Betting on an INTC turnaround worked very well for those who did so — over the last 12 months alone, Intel is up by more than 200%. With Intel trading in the high-$70s following the Q1 earnings release, it is now, by far, no longer a bargain stock — instead, it trades at the highest share price it has seen in recent years. By now, the company seems to be fully priced for a big recovery — but while things are moving in the right direction, Intel hasn't fully recovered yet. Based on that, I'm less bullish on INTC now compared to last fall, when shares traded at around $30, as the upside potential seems way more limited now, and since the downside risk, if anything goes wrong, is now likely a lot more elevated.

It is definitely possible that INTC continues to climb higher if good news continues to come in, especially if the market remains enthusiastic about semiconductor stocks in general, but I think INTC was a much better investment when there was more pessimism and less enthusiasm around the stock. Its Q1 results were surprisingly good, but they weren't a "20% after-hours price jump following a huge rally over the previous months" kind of good, I believe.

data.title: Unum: Careful About Investing In Insurance Right Now
data.description:
In this article, I'll be updating on Unum Group (UNM), a company I looked at about two months back. I want to reiterate some of the risks and challenges I saw and the valuation risks that are currently present in the stock at this price. The company currently trades at 9-10x normalized P/E. It now carries a BBB, investment-grade credit rating, but only a 2.38% yield. When I bought the company at a low price, it had an over 5% yield on cost at the time. Now, the yield isn't even at the level of the risk-free rate.

What this means is that the current valuation would require either substantial earnings growth or a massive premiumization to justify the price that some are calling for Unum to trade at. If you follow my work, you know that I don't typically value insurance above 8-13x P/E, and anything above that would be reserved for some of the best companies in the entire world.

Is Unum one of the best insurance businesses in the world?

I would argue that it is not.

I say that, based on its improvements, it's not as low as it once was. Some investors believe that the challenges with the LTC blocks are done. I'd say these recent challenges are over, and the opportunity brought about by their low valuation is also over. It also has a history of going heavily into corporate bonds during times of economic downturns, which gives it a higher volatility and risks.

All of the upsides in the company - AM Best rating of A for its health subsidiaries, market leadership in disability insurance (which is obviously attractive), and good earnings growth—unfortunately play second fiddle to what I would consider a lack of upside simply because there's very little reason for the company to trade higher than this.

Let me show you what I mean.

Unum: Overall Upside Would Require Substantial Growth Or Substantial Premiumization

There are two ways for Unum to significantly outperform from here on out. Since my last article over 2 months back, the company has not reported Q1 '26 yet but is about to in about a week (April 28th). This article will, therefore, work as an earnings preview, as well as a general update about the challenges I believe the company is facing, which makes any valuation above 8x P/E quite unlikely. I would say that investors should not be naive in this environment. No company will see substantial growth in valuation unless there's a very good reason for it. And Unum has not, for over 20 years, managed to trade consistently higher than 8-9x for a long- or medium-term period (2016-2017 highs do not count, in my view).

Why, you may ask, does Unum trade at such a substantial discount? After all, many insurers trade at 10-30% higher - so what makes the market so leery of this company's earnings trends?

It's fair to say that Unum's discount trends are well established. The industry average for insurance is about 1.6-1.9x to book, with the better ones enjoying a premium to their book value. Unum, meanwhile, often manages only 1.1x.

The mix is related to residual LTC risks and doubts about long-term growth. The risk for the LTC block is currently not over - the management for the company has successfully managed to significantly dial down the risk, which is excellent, but the segment still remains a source of significant volatility and risk. The reserves made for the risks (despite this block being closed) require a risk discount. The company is making some excellent money off of it, but the old saying that there's no "free lunch" applies. There's a reason the yield is so high.

Now there's the issue of the increased litigation pressure as well as increased cost and frequency for claims - not just for general insurers, but for Unum as well. This is especially visible in Unum's core U.S. segment results.

Here's something to understand if you want to get into Unum. The company focuses on disability insurance. Disability insurance, while an attractive business, is also an insurance business with a significantly higher incidence of claims.

This higher incidence of claims is due to a multitude of reasons, but examples include non-apparent conditions for the insured, such as mental health claims, back pain claims, and cancer claims, as opposed to more typical acute incident claims for other policies. These claims are also more common in these sectors because many of the disabled clients are prone to face a disabling condition prior to their retirement, related to things like chronic ailments.

In short, there's a reason why the company, with all of its LTC blocks and disability claims, is facing a much higher discount - and there's justification for it. Disabling conditions are subjective (often) rather than objective. Qualifying anxiety, chronic fatigue, or back pain is often something that is based on self-reporting. This has a net result for the insurer of longer claims. From a pure business perspective, Unum needs to be more careful and more defensive (in terms of claims litigation defense) simply because of the higher-risk demographics to which the company has exposure. This is why there are many attorneys that are specialized in handling the company's claims and why, again, there's more discounting in the company. Back when the company was UnumProvident, it accepted a $15M fine and had to reassess 200,000 denied claims.

None of these things makes Unum in any way a bad company. It just means that when investing in that company, you have to be careful that you're pricing the company for exactly this.

It's also why I'm taking such a reshaping of the closed block with a bit of salt. I don't doubt the company is seeing improved trends and improved growth rates - the question is if it's going to be enough.

Because while I have now been "harping" on the company's disability risks, that is also what Unum does. That's why I like Unum - at the right price. The company has decided to take on some of the riskier insurance clients, but at the right price, they also offer a significant upside for that. I think the expectation for an EPS growth rate of above 10% is a bit of wishful thinking, and I view the company's plan, as cited here, of share buybacks to be a bad idea given the valuation of the company and the typical valuation of where it should be.

Unum is diversifying away in order to expand its growth profile. Voluntary benefits, dental and vision insurance, and HR management are new growth vectors for the company. Unfortunately, I believe this is not exactly an advantage for the company. By diversifying away, the company is losing out against generalist broad-line insurers like MetLife (MET), which are far more competent at this sort of business. It's also business lines at much lower margins, requiring substantial investments into digitization, labor, and AI - all of which I believe are being underestimated in the current growth forecast. It also provides the company with a significantly higher interest rate sensitivity because, after all, the investments into fixed income securities remain.

If I were able to choose, I would say that Unum should focus on being the best at what it does - focus on the disability and become even better at it. It's obvious to me that the margins and earnings are there, even if I understand (and hopefully you too) that the risks are elevated, necessitating a higher discount.

What we have now is instead a more uncertain forecast due to volatility in growth and new areas for the business. I like this less. For Q1 '26, I expect the company's weaker underwriting performance to come home to roost - as was the Q4 '25 performance. Back in '25, the company went ahead and lowered its guidance from an original 6% growth to less than 1%. This is indicative of the same pressures that I have seen for some time. I, therefore, forecast the company will drop on earnings day.

That, coupled with the P/E, means I am not exactly optimistic about the potential here.

Valuation Or Unum - Unlikely To Be Very Good In Q1 '26

I've sold my shares in Unum at the time of writing. At the current time, the company trades at more than 9.3x P/E normalized, but the yield is, as I mentioned, sub-par. The 20-year average is 8.64x. Even if you assume that its new focus justifies the company at a higher multiple, I would argue that Unum has not yet proven that it actually deserves it. The forecast of 10-12% growth is unlikely to me, and I cite uncertainties in insurance as well as an almost 30% negative forecast growth accuracy to back this up. Also, even if the company did manage this, the upside at about 8-9x P/E would be less than 10% RoR, and that includes the dividend, on an annualized basis.

And again, this is if the company's highest-end forecasts come true. If we use an 8% forecast, a number the company has also used, this comes at the same P/E to less than 8.5% per year. There's just not that much upside in the stock. Current analyst estimates come to an average of $93/share, with a low of $80/share and a high of $113/share. This shows us that the analysts following the stock are actually, to a high degree, believing in the upside. However, despite this average, the company only has 6 of the analysts at a Buy, with the remaining 7 at either a Hold or a different rating. The implied valuation at the company's $93/share is over 11x P/E for the current earnings - it means that the analysts believe the company should trade at more of a standard insurance multiple - not something I agree with.

My latest price target, or PT, for the company was $52/share. I don't see a reason to change this at this time. For Q1 '26, I consider the company to not be a good buy given the valuation relative to the company's fundamentals (meaning the way its insurance sectors work). I will say that this company is a Hold, not a Buy, going into the first quarter.

Risks & Consideration

The primary risk to Unum is obviously not quality any longer, given its work with the LTC segment (residual risks remain, but nothing as we saw a few years back), but as of 2026, a valuation-related risk profile. At the current valuation, I do not see an upside that, based on either historical data or averages, could see a market-beating rate of return.

Combining inflation-related lack of upside (lower premium growth) with operational downsides from LTC block risks and adding to this valuation, I believe the likely upside here is meager. The company's new growth segments do not change my view here either. I would not continue to hold substantial amounts of Unum stock at this time.

Thesis

Unum is a theoretically appealing insurance company, but it no longer has a sufficiently attractive upside in terms of valuation. In my latest article in November, I rated this one a Hold, and irrespective of the coming Q1 results, I consider the risks to be weighing heavier than the upside here.

In a continued show of the validity of valuation, I am not shifting my long-term fair value and PT by much. I consider the company fairly valued at $52/share, around book value (adjusted for LTC block risks, updated for Q1 '26 and its estimates).

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this and a price target of $52/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a Hold rating as of April 2026.

data.title: Keurig Dr Pepper: A Beverage Portfolio Worthy Of Entry (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Keurig Dr Pepper Inc. (KDP) reported the company’s Q1 results on the 23rd of April. The beverage giant’s performance is split between a very strong refreshment beverage portfolio and a temporarily much weaker performance in the coffee business amid price volatility.

I believe that as coffee-related volatility should start to subside, and the market seems to price significant uncertainty regarding Keurig Dr Pepper’s separation into two pure-play companies, there is now an attractive entry point in the stock.

I initiated the stock at a Hold rating in my previous October 2025 article on the stock, titled “Keurig Dr Pepper: The Focus Is On JDE Peet's Integration.” The stock has since returned 5%, nearly on par with the S&P 500’s 6% return.

Keurig Dr Pepper Q1 Review

On the surface, Keurig Dr Pepper’s Q1 results were stable. Revenues came in at $3.98 billion, reflecting fairly good 8.1% constant currency revenue growth. Growth stayed consistent from 8.6% in 2025. The quarter’s $0.39 adjusted EPS declined by -7.1% year-on-year but still came close to market expectations. Both revenues and the EPS beat Wall Street’s consensus estimates slightly.

The underlying performance was again much more mixed; earnings growth was split between a great performance in U.S. refreshment beverages and a much slower performance in U.S. coffee and international markets.

As said, refreshment beverages clearly remain a bright spot for Keurig Dr Pepper. The segment’s sales grew by 11.9% to $2.60 billion, reflecting very good volume growth of 7.2% as well as 4.7% growth from pricing increases. Keurig Dr Pepper’s soft beverage brand portfolio has clearly continued to capture U.S. market share; The Coca-Cola Company’s (KO) latest earnings reported much slower 1% unit case volume growth in North America, PepsiCo, Inc. (PEP) reported a -2.5% organic North American beverage volume decline, and National Beverage Corp.'s (FIZZ) latest earnings reported a -0.3% revenue decline. All major peers have fared in comparison to Keurig Dr Pepper’s growth.

Adjusted operating income for the segment grew by 9.8% to $742 million, still reflecting some margin pressure. The adjusted operating margin declined by 60 basis points to 28.5%, as higher costs from aluminum tariffs and other cost inflation weighed on the industry. At the same time, Keurig Dr Pepper’s market share gains have required higher marketing expenses from the company.

Overall, I believe that a strong quarter sets up a confident outlook for Keurig Dr Pepper’s refreshment beverages despite a slight margin hiccup.

The coffee market has been much more challenging. The previous surge in coffee futures (KC1:COM) has raised costs immensely, pushing Keurig Dr Pepper to try to balance between margins and consumer demand when deciding price increases. There’s clear elasticity in consumer demand, leading to much less coffee consumption as prices have increased.

The Q1 report's financials still echoed coffee price turbulence. Keurig Dr Pepper’s U.S. coffee sales declined by -2.3% to $857 million, as a 5.9% pricing increase was met by an -8.2% decline in sales volume. Despite significant price hikes, the segment’s adjusted operating income declined by -21.3% to $199 million due to input cost inflation and other smaller factors. The adjusted operating margin declined by 560 basis points to 23.2%.

Luckily, the coffee segment’s financial performance should improve going forward. Coffee prices have moderated during late 2025 and early 2026, which should gradually start to show up in results as cheaper inventory starts to be sold. I don’t expect the U.S. coffee segment’s recovery to be instant, but a good earnings rebound should still happen with a delay. Keurig Dr Pepper needs to first cycle through more expensive inventory.

The JDE Peet's acquisition, completed on the 1st of April, should bring further potential to the coffee business. Keurig Dr Pepper targets $400 million in cost synergies as the businesses are integrated. The acquisition brings clear benefits from increased scale.

Finally, the international segment showed quite a mixed performance; sales grew by 8.5% in constant currency to $520 million due to pricing increases, but adjusted operating income declined by -15.1% to $87 million. Cost inflation and higher marketing expenses pressured the segment’s margins as Keurig Dr Pepper is trying to grow internationally. Similar coffee price concerns weighed on Keurig Dr Pepper’s international performance.

Overall, the report sets up good expectations for the rest of 2026. Keurig Dr Pepper reaffirmed the company’s 2026 guidance, suggesting revenues of $25.9-26.4 billion. The revenue guidance implies good 4-6% organic growth, as well as an $8.5-8.7 billion contribution from JDE Peet’s. The adjusted EPS is guided to grow in the low double-digit percentages, reflecting 4-6% organic growth and a 6-7 percentage point contribution from JDE Peet’s. Margins are guided to stabilize after a weaker Q1.

KDP Stock Seems Undervalued

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for Keurig Dr Pepper. I now consolidate JDE Peet’s earnings from Q2’2026 forward as the company was acquired and add the company’s cash and debt as of December 2025.

I expect revenue growth to be very high in 2026 and 2027 due to JDE Peet’s acquisition, but I also expect fair mid-single-digit organic growth underneath in the next few years. Sales synergies with JDE Peet’s and a strong refreshment beverage performance create a platform for elevated growth. I still ultimately expect growth to gradually slow down to just 1.5% alongside inflation.

Cost synergies with JDE Peet’s and declining coffee prices should raise margins in the next few years. I expect the EBIT margin to reach 18% in a few years.

I don’t assume Keurig Dr Pepper needs to invest too much to achieve the estimated level of growth; the company’s after-tax cash flow conversion should remain fairly good.

The estimates put Keurig Dr Pepper’s fair value at $41.5, suggesting 46% upside as of writing. The stock seems to be undervalued if earnings perform nearly as well as I expect—there's an attractive entry point in my opinion. Uncertainty regarding the JDE Peet’s acquisition and the following business separation seems to weigh too much on the market’s sentiment.

The forward adjusted P/E stands at only 11.7, compared to The Coca-Cola Company’s 23.1 P/E and PepsiCo’s 17.8 P/E.

The fair value estimate is up from $33.8 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital (WACC) of 6.06%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I now estimate a 5.79% interest rate, being 150 basis points above the current 10-year bond yield. I expect debt to remain quite high at a 40% long-term debt-to-equity ratio.

For the cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.29% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.18% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimate for the US. I have kept the beta estimate at 0.54. With a liquidity premium of 0.2%, the cost of equity stands at 6.75% and the WACC at 6.06%.

Takeaway

Keurig Dr Pepper reported fairly good, but varying, Q1 results. The company has continued to gain market share in U.S. refreshment beverages, but coffee price turbulence continues to weigh on the second largest segment. While international sales growth has been strong, cost inflation and marketing investments weigh on margins in international markets.

Overall, there’s reason to be optimistic about Keurig Dr Pepper’s future; coffee prices have started to stabilize, the JDE Peet’s acquisition should bring synergies, and the refreshment beverage outlook is strong. As KDP stock now seems clearly undervalued, I upgrade my rating to Buy.

data.title: RTX Corporation: Patience Will Be Rewarded
data.description:
Most of my analysis is focused on the tech sector, and so it's not a huge surprise that I haven't yet covered RTX Corporation (RTX). However, with the world seemingly becoming an increasingly dangerous place due to wars in Ukraine and the Middle East, RTX has become too important of a company to ignore any further. While we're currently in an environment that should suit RTX well, you can see below that the stock has seen not-so-great performance since the start of March. The defense contractor reported their latest earnings just a couple of days ago, and so today I have decided to initiate coverage.

Below, it is shown that the geopolitical landscape and defense spending trends are providing tailwinds for RTX. Demand is strong, as is evident by the rising backlog. The time needed to scale their production capacity is currently probably the largest risk to their near-term performance. That said, the long-term growth story is stronger than it has been in some time since defense spending trends triggered by wars in recent years are unlikely to die down even if the conflicts themselves end. With capacity expected to increase substantially over the coming decade or so, I believe investors should still find a decent amount of opportunity in RTX even if the valuation isn't cheap. Therefore, I'm initiating the stock at a Buy rating.

Surging Tomahawk Orders

Before getting into their earnings results, let's take a brief look at how the Iran war may be providing tailwinds for RTX's business. At this point, it is no secret that the conflict in the Middle East has used up a significant part of the United States' Tomahawk inventories. A little while ago, it was reported that the Navy is planning to purchase 785 Tomahawk missiles in 2027, up sharply from this year's 55. This is clearly driven by the Pentagon's need to restock, and so the tailwinds from the Iran war are clear.

Furthermore, with all eyes on the Strait of Hormuz, the United States is now trying to clear the mines that were set in the key waterway. RTX's Common Uncrewed Surface Vessel is one of the most important technologies being deployed, as it tows an advanced floating sonar that sweeps the seafloor. As of now, RTX's solutions are playing an integral part in the United States' Middle East mission, and with the war having no obvious end in sight, the company's importance cannot be overstated.

Growth Actually Slowed

For 2026 Q1, RTX reported sales of $22.1 billion, up 9% YoY. This was a deceleration from the previous quarter, and while this may seem to contradict the discussion above, it is important to realize that the company can't simply increase their production overnight. The tailwinds are there, but they also aren't going to translate into immediate top-line growth acceleration. Still, RTX beat analyst expectations by over $600 million, and so things could have been much worse.

In organic terms, growth slowed from Q4's 14% to 10% as a signal that current business activity isn't the strongest. Do note that both their Collins Aerospace and Pratt & Whitney segments saw 10% organic growth, while Raytheon saw a 9% increase. The fact that Raytheon relatively underperformed shows that key Tomahawk tailwinds have yet to kick in as of now. That's not a surprise.

Backlog Is Growing

With tailwinds needing time to show up in their top-line results, their order backlog numbers could be more indicative of what's to come for RTX. As shown above, for commercial aerospace, their backlog jumped by 30% YoY to $162 billion, while for their defense business, there was an 18% increase. Both growth rates clearly outpace the top-line growth rate for the company as a whole to show that demand is basically outstripping supply right now. For their Raytheon segment, while the Q1 book-to-bill ratio may have been a little softer than desired, the rolling 12-month book-to-bill was reported to be 1.48. That is a sign that there's no shortage in demand.

Capacity Expansion

In response to this buildup of backlog, the company is pursuing higher production capacity. In their earnings presentation it is stated that they're "investing to ramp up critical munitions over the next decade" and of course that includes Tomahawk. In the nearer term, it was reported on April 21st that Pratt & Whitney Canada had opened their manufacturing facility in Morocco, and so that should help to address the demand for aircraft engines.

Separately, it was reported just yesterday (April 22nd) that Pratt & Whitney is investing $100 million in Poland to boost production and add advanced capabilities. This is aimed at meeting demand for their engines, including the GTF, F135, and F100. At the end of the day, strong demand is encouraging, but capacity is needed for tailwinds to become meaningful. At this point, RTX seems focused on boosting production in a prudent manner.

Profitability Looks Solid

Just briefly, before we move to the outlook, profitability seems to be in a respectable position for the company as a whole. Adjusted operating margin saw YoY expansion in each of their three segments to show that the business is one that's well run. Raytheon led the way with around 150 basis points of expansion. This strong profitability clearly showed up on their bottom line as adjusted EPS climbed 21% YoY and also beat expectations by $0.26.

Full-Year Outlook

After beating top and bottom line expectations for Q1, RTX raised their full-year outlook for adjusted sales and for adjusted EPS, and of course that is welcome news for investors. Their organic sales growth rate projection remains unchanged, however, and the 5% to 6% range is a considerable slowdown from 2025's 11% increase. This indicates that the company's capacity expansion will take time and that the backlog to revenue conversion will perhaps be slower than what investors desire.

For adjusted EPS, the new midpoint implies growth of 8%, and so at least margins should continue to be strong moving forward. Despite the rise of the top and bottom line outlook, it should be noted that they still guided below consensus. Therefore, it seems that analysts thought tailwinds would translate into growth more quickly.

No Problem Securing Contracts

At this point, RTX is having no problems securing contracts. For example, on April 18th, it was reported that they, along with Lockheed Martin (LMT), had been contracted to sell $12 billion in naval systems to Germany. This will, of course, add to their backlog. Even more recently, RTX's Raytheon won a $213 million contract to upgrade the systems on the United States' Zumwalt-class vessels. Demand is clearly not the problem right now for their growth.

While demand outstripping supply is a much better problem to have than the other way around, the limits to scaling quickly are probably the largest risk to RTX's growth right now. If the company could deliver Tomahawk missiles now, there is little question that the Pentagon would buy them without hesitation. Therefore, moving forward, investors should keep these limitations in mind even if tailwinds get increasingly strong.

Valuation Analysis

While a forward P/E of 26.29 is on the high side for a company that's classified as an industrial company, I would say that the valuation is quite reasonable and should still provide long-term investors with a decent amount of opportunity. The recent pullback in the multiple from above 30 could be a nice entry point.

As stated above, the number one risk for RTX right now is capacity constraints. However, over the long run, the company is working to increase their production, and so investors can expect better backlog-to-revenue conversion over time. With the backlog totaling $271 billion right now and full-year 2025 revenues only having been $88.6 billion, it is clear that their business will be robust for the foreseeable future. While conflicts like the Iran war will end at some point, there is little doubt that the trends of increased global defense spending will continue as instability is seemingly on the rise.

With the long-term setup being as it is, I view RTX's relatively premium valuation as justified. The company can't meet all the demand in the near term, but with the growth story now being at its most solid point in recent memory, there are reasons to believe that the risk/reward is still favorable now.

Conclusion

RTX's stock performance actually hasn't been all that great recently as investors start to realize that demand can't be met in an easy manner. While this is a factor that short-term investors or traders should recognize, I believe for long-term investors, the recent pullback in the valuation provides a nice entry point. As capacity expansion leads to revenue and EPS growth acceleration over time, the stock will naturally move higher to maintain reasonable levels in their valuation multiples. With that in mind, I'm initiating RTX at a Buy rating, as outperformance is likely for patient investors.

data.title: Ford: Prepare For A Challenging Year Ahead (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Just when things seemed to be gradually improving for Ford (F), the company's management has confirmed that its electric vehicle strategy is not working. The announcement comes after a brief period of relief for Ford's investors after the Q3 2025 report.

A major restructuring was announced, and yet the market's response was relatively muted up until late February and early March, when forecasts about automotive sales were announced and it became clear that Ford's EV sales are collapsing.

While macroeconomic conditions are influencing all automotive OEMs, Ford's investors are in a far worse position when compared to those who invested in General Motors (GM) or Toyota (TM). F stock is up by less than 6% for the past 5-year period, and after accounting for inflation in that period, real return is in negative territory.

For years now, F investors have been awaiting a proper turnaround by relying on the stock's ridiculously low valuation. Even when compared to GM and Toyota, Ford's stock stands out. The price-to-sales multiple of 0.27 could easily be considered excessively low.

On a non-GAAP forward earnings basis, Ford is priced at a single-digit multiple, which is a rare sight in today's equity market.

The problem is that recent events at the company have shown that even these low multiples might not be enough to shelter shareholders from a potential future disaster at the company.

Never-Ending Problems

Ford's full fiscal year 2025 results were largely in line with expectations, and the market did not react negatively to the significant cost charges throughout the period. While we should recognize the difficulties that all legacy OEMs face, it is still hard to forego the trend in Ford's operating earnings over the past decade or so.

Profitability has gone nowhere over the past decade, with 2025 marking the second year in this 10-year timeframe when Ford reported significant GAAP losses. The first time this happened in 2020, the whole economy faced major challenges around the pandemic lockdowns, but in 2025 the problem is company-specific.

Around $4bn of cost headwinds in 2025 were outside of Ford's management control, with the aluminum supplier Novelis experiencing a major fire in one of its plants and tariffs weighing further on costs.

(...) $2 billion headwind from Novelis fires and the net tariff impact of $2 billion. That's a $1 billion higher tariff impact than we communicated just in October due to the unexpected and late year change in tariff credits for auto parts

Source: Ford Q4 2025 Earnings Transcript

The impact of such cost items on share price performance is often limited within the short term, as market participants tend to disregard one-off cost headwinds.

On an adjusted basis, Ford's EV segment even appears to be doing quite well, with sales up significantly in FY 2025 and operating losses narrowing down.

The harsh reality, however, is that the management has recognized that its EV strategy is not working. A strategy that has been promoted for many years is now coming under scrutiny and would most likely result in sluggish sales, more losses in the coming years, and high restructuring costs.

The extract from Ford's most recent annual SEC filing speaks for itself and could be a precursor to years of automotive segment losses as the company pivoted heavily towards EVs.

The challenges facing the electric vehicle (“EV”) market that have led to lower-than-anticipated adoption rates have, in turn, led us to conclude, in the fourth quarter of 2025, that a path to long-term profitability for our EV business was not possible without taking strategic actions. Accordingly, in December 2025, we made the decision to rationalize our EV manufacturing capacity and product roadmap by cancelling three previously planned EVs and ending production of the current generation F-150 Lightning EV.

As a result of the challenges facing the EV market and the decisions we made in response to those challenges, in the fourth quarter of 2025, we tested our Model e segment long-lived assets for impairment and recorded a pre-tax charge of $8.1 billion in Cost of sales, representing the amount by which the carrying value of these assets exceeded the estimated fair value. We primarily used the market and cost approaches to estimate fair value for our long-lived assets.

Source: Ford 10-K SEC Filing

The aforementioned $8bn impairment charge was not all, with significant additional charges being realized in 2025 in relation to cancellations and contractual commitments also weighing on profitability.

In addition to the charge described above, in the fourth quarter of 2025, as part of Cost of sales, we recognized asset write-downs of $1.1 billion for assets related to the EV program cancellations referenced above; recognized $1.2 billion of other charges, primarily related to contractual commitments related to those programs; and fully impaired Model e segment goodwill of $0.2 billion

Source: Ford 10-K SEC Filing

To make matters worse, competition in the EV space is not sleeping and, in fact, seems to be intensifying. Just yesterday, Tesla (TSLA) reported its Q1 2026 results, and Elon Musk made clear that the company is entering into an investment cycle that will include a notable pickup in investments and capital expenditures.

All that is now likely to become a major threat for Ford going forward, as the company was already struggling with achieving high profitability in its automotive segment and relied heavily on Ford Credit to fill up the hole in its bottom line. Not to mention that Ford's restructuring and a potential shift in its EV strategy comes at a time when the U.S. consumer is coming under significant pressure and consumer confidence has collapsed to record low levels.

Expect Cash Flow Pressures

Even when we disregard the non-cash impairment charges in 2025, things still look very grim for Ford. In recent years, the company's cash flow from operations has been barely sufficient to cover the company's ongoing capital expenditure and dividend outlays. This has left very little leeway for more growth capex, strategic acquisitions, deleveraging, dividend increases, and share buybacks.

As we could see on the graph below, this trend seems to have changed in 2025, but unfortunately this is likely to be short-lived.

On the graph below, we once again have Ford's annual cash flow from operations plotted on an annual basis, but this time against the annual change in working capital.

As opposed to 2023 and 2024, when the overall impact of working capital was negligible, in 2025 we had a massive positive cash inflow due to changes in working capital. This was caused by a drop in finance receivables and a significant increase in accounts payable and other current liabilities (see the table below).

The lower amount of finance receivables is concerning when it comes to Ford's revenue growth and, in particular, the expansion of Ford Credit. The $10bn positive impact from higher accounts payable and other liabilities, on the other hand, is highly unlikely to be sustained if topline growth remains low. At present, Ford's expected revenue growth rate stands below 1%, which means that investors should anticipate a much lower working capital tailwind in 2026.

Conclusion

Although the market was prepared for the disappointing results in 2025, Ford's most recent earnings release has shown that investors should not anticipate a turnaround anytime soon. The upcoming Q1 earnings release next week will shed some light on what's in store for 2026, but a necessary change in EV strategy could mean a few more years of pain for Ford's shareholders. This comes at a time when the U.S. consumer will face significant pressures in spending and Ford's cash flow is likely to shrink in 2026. As a result, even the record low valuation multiples can't justify the risks involved in holding Ford's stock.

data.title: ON Semiconductor: Chip Stocks' Parabolic Rise Draws Concerns
data.description:
Semiconductor stocks are working on a perfect April (as of this writing). Trading higher for 17 days running, the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index (SOX) reached a record winning streak as the AI-led bull market simply went into another gear. The early-2026 decline feels like a distant memory, and investors now await key chip-maker earnings.

One of these market leaders is ON Semiconductor (ON). The stock has soared by more than 75% since its March 30 low. The stock jumped 9% on Thursday, April 23, following an upgrade out of B. Riley, along with bullish trends in Intel (INTC) and (more so) a stunning post-reporting climb in Texas Instruments (TXN).

As for ON, I have a "Hold" rating. The valuation is not extreme, but it’s no longer a bargain. The chart, meanwhile, has gone dangerously parabolic, with the $111 record high not far above the current price.

ON & SMH Going Parabolic As Earnings Season Progresses, Software Slides

Back in February, ON reported a mixed set of quarterly results. Q4 non-GAAP EPS of $0.64 topped the Wall Street consensus forecast of $0.62, while revenue of $1.53 billion, down 12% from the same period last year, was a small $10 million miss. Its non-GAAP gross margin came in at 38.2%, while the operating margin verified at 19.8%.

Shares rose 3.5% in the session that followed (after an initial dip), which was actually the best earnings reaction going back to July 2024. Looking ahead to the May 4 Q1 report, the options market prices in a high 9.5% earnings-related stock price swing based on the at-the-money straddle expiring soonest after the release. Shares of the $35 billion market cap Semiconductors industry company feature extremely high implied volatility of 77.9%.

Looking back on the quarter that was, ON’s net sales dipped 1% sequentially, but revenue rose in its Automotive, Industrial, and AI Data Center segments. The company also tallied record free cash flow of $1.4 billion (a 24% FCF margin) and AI data center revenue exceeding $250 million. The management team noted progress in its expansion of the Treo platform, which reached a design-win funnel of over $1 billion. A large buyback shouldn't go unnoticed, given the authorization of a new $6 billion share repurchase program.

Color On Quarter

As for expectations for the Q1 report due out in just a couple of weeks, ON sees revenue ranging between $1.435 and $1.535 billion, along with a non-GAAP gross margin in the range of 37.5% and 39.5%. According to the Q1 presentations, its non-GAAP diluted EPS is forecast between $0.56 and $0.66.

The company's long-term outlook remains focused on high-growth secular trends, targeting a 10% to 12% revenue CAGR and a significant expansion of gross margins to 53%. Those are high bars to hit, so this upcoming quarterly report could be a bit of a show-me story after the big stock price ascent. Big picture, ON continues shedding non-core assets (something the B.
